text: The video demonstrates the construction of an ileorectal anastomosis using a single-port laparoscopic technique. The surgeon mobilizes the end ileostomy and places the anvil of a circular stapler into its lumen. The distal ileum is returned into the peritoneum, and an Alexis wound protector is positioned at the stoma site. A glove is attached to the outer ring of the wound protector, with laparoscopic trocars inserted into the glove fingers, converting the stoma site into a single port portal.

The laparoscopic view through the surgical glove port reveals the distal colorectal stump, previously buried subfascially. The surgeon uses a 30-degree video scope for enhanced visualization and employs a ligature device along with downward traction to efficiently mobilize the colorectal stump.

The surgeon uses a sixty millimeter laparoscopic stapler to cross-staple the colorectal stump, ensuring a fresh, well-vascularized end for the ileorectal anastomosis. The stapler is inserted through one of the glove port fingers and fired to fully transect the stump. After transection, the cuff of the colorectum is withdrawn into an unused finger of the glove port, which is then clamped off with the specimen inside to maintain pneumoperitoneum.

The surgeon assesses the length of the distal ileum with the anvil in place to ensure it can reach the top of the distal colorectal stump for a tension-free anastomosis. Observing limited mobility, the surgeon performs further dissection, carefully examining the small bowel and dividing interloop adhesions between the bowel and its mesentery to restore mobility.

The surgeon passes the head of the circular stapler per anode, positioning its spike just posterior to the cross staple line. The stapler head is then connected to the spike of the anvil stapler, constructing the anastomosis under direct endoscopic vision. A sigmoidoscope is used to test the integrity of the anastomosis by descending it with air to check for air bubble leakage. Finally, the stoma site incision is closed, concluding the operation.
